coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of functionalized carbon dots for the ultrasensitive detection of Escherichia coli and iron (III)
coredata.dc:creator: Chandra, Soumen
Mahto, Triveni Kumar
Chowdhuri, Angshuman Ray
Das, Balaram
Sahu, Sumanta kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report a single step solid phase synthesis strategy for colistin modified fluorescent carbon dots (CDs@colistin) for the selective detection of gram-negative bacteria like Escherichia coli. Colistin is a well known cyclic polypeptide antibiotic drug but here we have used as a binding ligand for E. coli. Here also, the synthesised CDs@colistin are used for selective Fe3+ metal ion detection. The detection limits are 460cfu/mL for E. coli and 56nM for Fe3+ with a linear range of 3.81×102–2.44×104 cfu/mL and 0–48μM, respectively. The synthesised CDs@colistin are well dispersed in water with an uniform particle size ranging from 2nm to 5nm executing high quantum yield of 7.56%. The synthesis process of CDs@colistin is simple, cheap, and less time consuming. Simultaneously, we have applied CDs@colistin as a novel probe for E. coli detection in three different real samples like human urine, apple juice, and tap water to evaluate the practical applicability of the synthesised colistin conjugated carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid phase synthesis
Colistin
Fe3+ ion detection
E. coli detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ion by CDs@colistin-3
Detection of E. coli in real samples
Zeta potential measurement of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs
CDs@colistin-3 as probes for Fe3+ sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of the precursor colistin, ammonium di hydrogen citrate, CDs@colistin-3, and CDs; (b) HRTEM image of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3; (c) FESEM image of CDs@colistin-3; (d) The particle size distribution of CDs@colistin-3.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs@colistin-3; (b) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s region; (c) High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s region; (d) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s region.

(a) PL-spectrum at 350nm excitation and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs; (b) PL- spectra of the CDs at different excitation (300 nm–450nm); (c) PL-spectrum at 360nm excitation and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@colistin-3; (d) PL-spectra of the CDs@colistin-3 at different excitation (300 nm- 450nm).

(a) The plot of F/F0 in presence of 1mM different metals ion; (b) The quenching of PL- emission spectra of CDs@colistin-3 at 360nm excitation with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ion (0–270μM); (c) The plot of (F0-F)/F0 versus different concentration of Fe3+ ion (0–270μM), inset – the straight line plot of F0/F versus concentration of Fe3+ ion (0–48μM); (d) The effect of mixture of cations on the luminescence quenching of CDs@colistin-3 at 360nm excitation.

Fluorescence spectra of different concentration of E. coli labeled with CDs@colistin-1(a); CDs@colistin-2 (c); CDs@colistin-3 (e); CDs@colistin-4 (g) at 360nm excitation, The linear plot of fluorescence intensity at 360nm excitation against different concentration of E. coli labeled with CDs@colistin-1 (b); CDs@colistin-2 (d); CDs@colistin-3 (f); CDs@colistin-4 (h).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of E. coli (1×108 cfu/mL) and S. aureus (1×108 cfu/mL) labeled with CDs@colistin-3 at 360nm excitation (b) Fluorescence emission spectra at of E. coli bacterial cell (1×108 cfu/mL) labeled with CDs@colistin-3 and CDs 360nm excitation.
Fluorescent image of E. coli. (a, b, c) and S. aureus (d, e, f) labeled with CDs@colistin-3 at two different excitation [excitation wave length 450–480nm, 510–550nm].

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of functionalized carbon dots for the ultrasensitive detection of Escherichia coli and iron (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology and Microbiology Laboratory, Department of Human Physiology with Community Health, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore, 721102, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Human Physiology with Community Health

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report a single step solid phase synthesis strategy for colistin modified fluorescent carbon dots (CDs@colistin) for the selective detection of gram-negative bacteria like Escherichia coli. Colistin is a well known cyclic polypeptide antibiotic drug but here we have used as a binding ligand for E. coli. Here also, the synthesised CDs@colistin are used for selective Fe3+ metal ion detection. The detection limits are 460cfu/mL for E. coli and 56nM for Fe3+ with a linear range of 3.81×102–2.44×104 cfu/mL and 0–48μM, respectively. The synthesised CDs@colistin are well dispersed in water with an uniform particle size ranging from 2nm to 5nm executing high quantum yield of 7.56%. The synthesis process of CDs@colistin is simple, cheap, and less time consuming. Simultaneously, we have applied CDs@colistin as a novel probe for E. coli detection in three different real samples like human urine, apple juice, and tap water to evaluate the practical applicability of the synthesised colistin conjugated carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli (E. coli) is one of the most dangerous pathogenic bacteria, which is responsible for many types of diseases like abdominal pain, fatigue, diarrhea, septicemia, hemolytic uremic syndrome, and even kidney failure [1,2]. The existence of E. coli in drinking water and foodstuffs is a chronic worldwide problem. In the last decades, detection of E. coli using nanomaterials is a hot research topic in the field of biosensors [3–5]. Although, conventional methods for the detection of E. coli are selective and sensitive, but they are too slow from the perspective of industrial needs. Driven by the need, in this work we have developed a nanomaterial based carbon dots for selective E. coli detection.

In the last few years, numerous advanced analytical approaches have been developed for rapid detection of E. coli, such as flow cytometry [6], surface enhanced raman scattering [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [8], polymerase chain reaction [9], ampero-metric immune sensor [10], and fluorescent methods [11]. Among them, fluorescent method has drawn a lot of attention due to the excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, non destructiveness, and low cost [12]. To date, however, diverse types of fluorescent materials (fluorescence nanoparticles and organic dye) have been developed for E. coli detection. For example Chen et al. synthesised dye doped fluorescent silica nanoparticles for selective and rapid detection of pathogenic E. coli O157:H7 using fluorescence microscopy [13]. Si et al. used novel pH-sensitive and water soluble fluorescent organic nanoparticles for selective and accurate real-time detection of bacterial growth [14]. However, the fluorescent materials synthesized above usually consisted of tedious multistep synthetic route. In this context we have developed a functionalized carbon dots and a fluorescent nanoparticles in a single step for E. coli detection.

Nowadays, carbon dots (CDs), the promising and fascinating fluorescent materials, have attracted considerable attention in a wide range of applications due to their unique properties such as high chemical stability, water solubility, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and lack of blinking [15–17]. The detail achievements of CDs reported in the last five years have been reviewed by Luo et al., Park et al. and Liu et al. [18–20]. Apart from the diverse applications, functionalized CDs have been used for selective E. coli detection, which is one of the high priorities for the current world. For example, Nandi et al. reported bacterial detection and imaging based on the attachment of amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) to bacterial cells [21]. Wang et al. synthesised fluorescence carbon dots from papaya, as a green carbon source for the detection of E. coli [22]. Bhaisare et al. developed magnetic carbon dots using magnetic nanoparticles and chitosan for pathogen detection [23]. We have also developed a convenient method to construct fluorescent carbon dots by embedding amikacin for selective E. coli detection [24]. All reported functionalized CDs were developed by direct immobilization of CDs with biomolecules for bacteria detection. Although these functionalized carbon dots have high sensitivity towards selective E. coli detection. However the poor synthesis scale (about 2–3mg at a time) is a major flaw which actually restricts their practical applications. So there are very limited reports of the fabrication of functionalized CDs in pilot scale for E. coli detection [25], additional surface modification of CDs by other biomolecules are often required. Simultaneously, researchers have been focused on the fabrication of CDs in a single step for diverse applications [26,27]. In this context, we have developed CDs for selective E. coli detection and Fe3+ ions sensing with favourable properties like (a) pilot scale synthesis, (b) facile way, and (c) using chief chemicals. Meanwhile, it is well known that Fe3+ plays a vital role in chemical and biological processes in living organism [28–31]. However, excessive Fe3+ in human body causes serious diseases like Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson's disease, liver and kidney damage. Therefore, the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ is an important issue in biological systems [32].

Colistin (also known as polymyxin E) is used as an effective antibiotic against multi-resistant gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Acinetobacter [33], which are the leading causes of infections. Colistin interacts to lipopolysaccharide and phospholipids in the outer cell membrane of Gram-negative bacteria resulting in cytoplasmic leakage and cell death [34,35]. The detail properties of bacterial lipopolysaccharide have been extensively characterized by various physical methods. But recently, colistin is not only used as an antibacterial agent but also used as a gram-negative bacteria detection i.e., E. coli. Carolina et al. developed colistin-functionalised CdSe/ZnS quantum dots for the detection of E. coli [36]. However, synthesis of such nanoparticle follow multistep as well as tedious synthetic route and hence not convenient to be used in routine detection analysis. In this context we have tried to develop colistin conjugated carbon dots in a single step for E. coli detection.

In this work, we have developed a simple bottom-up approach to synthesize colistin conjugated carbon dots (CDs@colistin) through carbonization of ammonium citrate in solid form in the presence of colistin. The conjugation of colistin in carbon dots has been confirmed by FTIR, NMR and Mass spectroscopy analysis. The morphology and size of the CDs was characterized by FESEM and TEM respectively. The physicochemical properties of functionalized CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs is proposed in order to achieve the sensitive and rapid detection of E. coli in aqueous suspension and real samples. Fluorescence spectra show a linear relationship with low detection limits for E. coli.

All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification throughout the experiments. Di ammonium hydrogen citrate (98%), di Sodium hydrogen phosphate (98.1%), chromium chloride, copper (II) sulphate, cadmium sulphate, and nickel (II) sulphate were purchased from Loba Chemie (India). Colistin Sulfate (mixture) was purchased from TCI chemicals. Iron (III) chloride, potassium chloride (99.5%), sodium chloride (≥99%), potassium di hydrogen phosphate (≥98%), and ethyl alcohol were acquired from Merck India. Nutrient broth and Luria broth were provided by Himedia. Gram negative (Escheria coli) and Gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria were obtained from Microbiology laboratory of Midnapore Medical College and Hospital (West Bengal). The bacterial cultures were carried out at 37°C throughout the experiment. Millipore water was used for all the experiment.

The carbon dots were synthesised in solid phase by simple pyrolysis method. In brief, 20mg of di ammonium hydrogen citrate was grind in mortar pestle, taken in a silica crucible and then simply heated at 180°C for 1h in hot air oven. After reaction, the black material was collected and dissolved in 10mL of water. Finally, the brownish solution of carbon dots was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 10min to reject large particles. The final brown coloured solution was achieved and dialysed with 3500Da dialysis bag in deionised water for overnight.

The colistin conjugated carbon dots were synthesised by same pyrolysis method. We have synthesised four types of colistin conjugated carbon dots for selective labeling of gram negative bacteria (E. coli) by varying the amount of colistin precursor and these were labelled as CDs@colistin-1, CDs@colistin-2, CDs@colistin-3 and CDs@colistin-4. In brief, 20mg of di ammonium hydrogen citrate was separately grind with 0.005, 0.01, 0.015 and 0.02mol of colistin sulphate by mortar pestle. Finally the fine powder was heated at 180°C for 1h in hot air oven. After reaction, the each black material was collected and dissolved separately in 10mL of Millipore water. Each brownish solution of CDs@colistin was centrifuged separately at 10,000 RPM for 10min to reject large particles. The final brown coloured solution was collected and dialysed with 3500Da dialysis bag in Millipore water for overnight. After synthesis, all CDs@colistin were treated for bacterial detection as well as Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ion by CDs@colistin-3
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the selective detection performance for Fe3+ ions by CDs@colistin-3, the fluorescence quenching performance was carried out with different metals ions. For this, CDs@colistin-3 was mixed with each of 300μM concentrated metals ion solution (Ca2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+). After 60min of reaction, the PL spectra were recorded at 360nm excitation. For detection of the selective quenching efficiency of CDs@colistin-3 photoluminescence sensor for Fe3+, sensing assay was performed in milipore water. In a typical assay, 10mL CDs@colistin-3 (1μg/mL) was dispersed in 100mL of milipore water. The quenching performance of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 was detected by the addition of 0.1mL finally diluted carbon dots solution in different concentrations of Fe3+ ion. The PL spectra were recorded at 360nm excitation after the reaction for 60min. All the above experiments were performed at room temperature.

To identify the selective detection of the gram negative bacteria, both gram negative bacteria (E. coli) and gram positive (S. aureus) live bacterial cell were chosen. Both the gram negative and gram positive bacteria were cultured in sterile clean room. First, all apparatus for bacteria cell culture were sterilized in an autoclave at high temperature and high pressure to achieved bacteria free clean apparatus. Sterile Luria–Bertani (LB) media [containing bacto-tryptone (2.0g), bacto-yeast extract (1.0g) and NaCl (1.0g) in deionised water (200mL)] was used to culture the E. coli and S. aureus bacteria. To culture the E. coli and S. aureus bacteria, a portion of each colony of E. coli and S. aureus bacteria was lifted from LB agar plates and incubated in 5mL LB media. The cultures of E. coli and S. aureus were allowed to grow in an incubator-shaker at 37°C. After overnight shaking in incubator-shaker, each type of bacteria was achieved to a stationary phase. Finally, 1mL from each cultured media was centrifuged at 10000 RPM for 10min to remove the supernatant and washed four times with PBS (13.6mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl,1.003mM Na2HPO4, 0.176mM KH2PO4, in 100mL deionised water; adjusted to pH7.4 using 0.1M HCl) and suspended in 1mL of PBS.

Each type of bacteria was again diluted with PBS buffer solution to adjust their absorbance approximately 1 at optical density 600 [25]. Diluted 1mL of 108 colony forming units (cfu) E. coli bacterial cells were again diluted to a concentration of 5×107 to 3.81×102 cfu per mL for further experimental study. 1mL of different concentration (5×107 to 3.81×102 cfu per mL) of E. coli cell was incubated separately with 0.1mL CDs@colistin-1 (1mg/mL), CDs@colistin-2 (1mg/mL), CDs@colistin-3 (1mg/mL) and CDs@colistin-4 (1mg/mL). After addition of carbon dots, the each concentration of bacteria cell was suspended by simple vortexing for 5min. The suspended each concentration of E. coli bacteria cells were kept under gentle shaking with orbital shaker at 200 RPM for 60min at room temperature. After shaking, the CDs@colistin attached E.coli cells were isolated from suspension by centrifuge at 8000 RPM for 10min and washed with PBS for further fluorescence emission study. Finally, the CDs@colistin attached E. coli bacteria cells were resuspended in PBS buffer and fluorescence spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer at excitation wavelength of 360nm.

To examine the CDs@colistin-3 attachment on the selective bacterial cells, CDs@colistin-3 (1mg/mL) was incubated with E. coli and S. aureus. The concentration of E. coli and S. aureus was adjusted to 106 cfu/mL. Each bacteria was incubated in a shaking incubator at 37°C for overnight. Both E. coli and S. aureus were washed twice with phosphate buffered saline to remove any unutilized CDs@colistin-3. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@colistin-3 was analyzed under fluorescent microscope for detection of fluorescence intensity in bacterial cells. The fluorescent images reveal that E. coli. labeled with CDs@colistin-3 has higher fluorescence intensity compared to S. aureus at two different excitations [excitation wave length for green 450–480nm and red 510–550nm].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E. coli in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli bacterial cell at the level of concentration 1×108 cfu/mL was incubated to three real samples including human urine, apple juice, and tap water to investigate the practical applicability of the synthesised colistin conjugated carbon dots to the real sample. The each 1mL of real sample (human urine, apple juice, and tap water) containing 1×108 cfu/mL E. coli bacterial cell was again diluted to a concentration of 5×107 to 3.81×102 cfu per mL with PBS buffer solution. Each sample was incubated with 0.1mL of CDs@colistin-3 (1mg/mL) and allowed to shake in an orbital shaker at 200 RPM for 60min. After shaking, CDs@colistin-3 labelled E. coli was separated from the real samples by centrifuge at 8000 RPM for 10min and washed 4 times with PBS (pH 7.4). Finally the CDs@colistin labelled E. coli bacterial cell was resuspended in 1mL of PBS and fluorescence spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer with excitation wavelength of 360nm.

All the functional groups present at the precursors as well as CDs@colistin-3 were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet Nexus FTIR) (model 870). The size and morphology of the CDs@colistin-3 were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and field emission electron microscopy (FESEM) [JEOL 3010 Japan- for HRTEM analysis and Phillips CM 200, Japan- for FESEM analysis]. Emission spectra of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs were recorded at different excitation by Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer, 120V; Perkin-Elmer 950 spectrometer was used to perform UV–vis absorption spectrum. The attachment of the colistin to the CDs@colistin-3 surface was confirmed by FT NMR Spectrometer model Avance-II (Bruker) with frequency 400MHz. The zeta potentials (ζ) of the CDs@colistin-3 and CDs were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments and Worcestershire, UK).

Colistin conjugated CDs can be easily synthesised by simple heat treatment with di ammonium hydrogen citrate and colistin sulphate. Colistin is a well-known old cyclic polypeptide antibiotic also called polymyxin E [36]. Previously reported papers show that the immense interest towards colistin due to its significant activity against multi-resistant gram-negative bacteria. Colistin is a bactericidal drug that interacts with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) molecules in the outer membrane of E. coli bacteria cell [36,35]. The interaction leading to displacement of divalent cations from the phosphate groups of membrane lipids. The displacement of the cation and interaction of the colistin drug to the E. coli bacteria cell stabilize the LPS membrane of the bacterial cell [36].

Among CDs@colistin-1, CDs@colistin-2, CDs@colistin-3, and CDs@colistin-4; CDs@colistin-3 shows very good response towards E. coli detection. Therefore, our detailed study is focused on the CDs@colistin-3 only.

The functional groups of the precursor, and CDs@colistin-3 were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The two broad peaks at around 3473cm−1 and 3170cm−1, indicating the OH stretching vibration of carboxylic acid, hydroxyl functional group as well as NH stretching vibration of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 [37]. The coexistence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of the CDs@colistin-3 is also supported by zeta potential experiments with a value of −21.8mV at neutral pH. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs@colistin-3, peak at 1680cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration mode of amide [38]. The shifting of CO starching frequency (at 1728cm−1) of ammonium citrate to the CO stretching frequency (at 1680cm−1) of CDs@colistin-3 indicates the formation of amide types bond at the surface of the CDs. Simultaneously, peak at 1410cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of CN bond [39]. Slightly shifting of the carbonyl stretching frequency at 1706cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of CDs from the carbonyl stretching vibration of CDs@colistin-3 indicates the attachment of colistin to the surface of the CDs. Further, the attachment of the colistin to the surface of the CDs was confirmed by 1HNMR and HR-Mass spectroscopy of the CDs@colistin-3 as well as the precursors.

The particle size and morphology of the as synthesised CDs@colistin-3 were investigated by HRTEM as shown in Fig. 1(b). HRTEM image indicates that CDs@colistin-3 is uniform in size with good spherical morphology. To draw the particles size distribution histogram, hundred particles are chosen from Fig. 1(b). The particle size distribution histogram is shown in Fig. 1(d). The particle size distribution indicates that CDs@colistin-3 are uniform with the average particle size distribution ranging from 2nm to 5nm. Maximum CDs@colistin-3 has size of 3.5nm. FESEM image of the as synthesised CDs@colistin-3 is shown in Fig. 1(c). Image clearly reveals that CDs@colistin-3 possesses spherical morphology.

The surface composition and chemical environments of the as synthesised CDs@colistin-3 were identified by XPS spectra. In XPS survey spectrum of CDs@colistin-3, the peaks at 283.28eV, 399.25eV, and 530.39eV correspond to the elements of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [40], as shown in Fig. 2 (a). This result indicates that the CDs@colistin-3 is composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements with the atomic percentages of 41.1%, 46.3%, and 13.6%, respectively. The high resolution scan of the C1s region corresponding the four different chemical environments is shown in Fig. 2(b). The four different peaks in the C1s spectrum corresponding to the sp2 carbon for CC at 284.5eV, sp3 CN at 285.2eV, CO at 286.8eV and OCO at 288.6eV [41]. The high resolution spectra of N1s corresponds to two different chemical environments for CNC at 398.3eV and NH at 400.2eV respectively [42] [Fig. 2(c)]. Simultaneously, the high resolution scans of the O1s region indicating the two different chemical environments for COH/COC at 531.9eV and CO at 532.9eV [Fig. 2(d)] The XPS spectra reveals that CDs@colistin-3 dots are highly nitrogen and oxygen reached and consequently resulting high fluorescence.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and Fig. 3(c). Fig. S5 represents that both CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are brownish colour in visible light but show bright blue fluorescence emission when brought into UV light at 365nm wavelength. The UV–vis absorption peak of CDs@colistin-3 at 340nm with a tail extending into the visible range ascribed to n-π* transition of the CO bond. While, the UV–vis absorption peak of synthesised CDs centred at 337nm attributed to n-π* transition of the CO bond [43,44]. The red shifting of absorption peak from 337nm to 340nm, as observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@colistin-3, is due to the attachment of colistin to the CDs surface. The broad peak at 340nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 attributed to the trapping of large extent of excitation energy. The trapping of excitation energy results huge amount of emission at 360nm excitation in PL-emission spectra [45]. Further, the optical properties of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 and CDs were compared with different excitation wavelengths. The PL emission spectra of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs at different excitation wavelengths starting from 300nm to 450nm are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (d). The maximum PL intensity was observed at 350nm excitation wavelengths for CDs and 360nm excitation wavelengths for CDs@colistin-3. The shifting of maximum excitation wavelength of CDs@colistin-3 compared to CDs is due to the attachment of colistin on the surface of CDs. The excitation-independent PL behaviour is related to the less surface defects and more uniform size of CDs@colistin-3, which is consistent with HRTEM observation. The PL-emission peak of CDs@colistin-3 shifts from 446nm to 510nm, while the excitation wavelength changes from 300nm to 450nm with the maximum emission peak centred at 447nm. Similarly, the emission peak of CDs shifts from 430nm to 512nm, while the excitation wavelength changes from 300nm to 450nm with the maximum emission peak centred at 440nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs were calculated and compared to each other. The FWHM of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 at 360nm excitation is about 85nm and CDs at 350nm excitation 79nm. The lower FWHM value of CDs compared to FWHM of CDs@colistin-3 attributed to the narrower particle size distribution of CDs [46]. The larger particle size of CDs@colistin-3 attributed to the passivation of colistin to the surface of the CDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of synthesised CDs@colistin-3 and CDs was measured to be about 7.56% and 11.21% with respect to quinine sulphate in 0.1M sulfuric acid solution (quantum yield 54%) as the standard. The decrement of quantum yield compared to CDs attributed to the presence of colistin on the surface of carbon dots. The presence of colistin on the surface of carbon dots may be interfering the radiative recombination of the excitions giving rise to lower fluorescence of CDs@colistin-3 in contrast to CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs@colistin-3 was calculated to be 7.9ns according to the fluorescence decay with excitation and emission wavelengths in 337 and 437nm shown in Fig. S6 (a). The short lifetime of CDs@colistin-3 is indicative of the radiative recombination of the excitons giving rise to fluorescence [47].

The variation of fluorescence intensities of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs with respect to pH are compared to each other, as shown in Fig. S4(a). The obtained result reveals that both CDs@colistin-3 and CDs show low fluorescence intensity both at very low pH and very high pH. However, the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged at pH 5 to pH 9. The decrement of the fluorescence intensity at very low pH and very high pH indicates that H+ and OH− ions interfere with the radiactive recombination of excitons [48]. In addition, the negatively charged functional group at the surface of CDs is also neutralized by free H+ in medium. Thus the neutral CDs come closer to each other, resulting decrement of florescence intensity. The negatively charged surface of CDs is also confirmed by zeta potential measurement, as shown in Fig. S5. The effect of ionic strength of the medium on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are shown in Fig. S4(b). The fluorescence intensity of both CDs@colistin-3 and CDs is intact even in high ionic strength. The effect of UV irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@colistin-3 was compared with commercial dye quinine, as shown in Fig. S4(c). The change of the negligible amount of fluorescent intensity of CDs@colistin-3 after 140min UV light irradiation indicating that CDs@colistin-3 has greater fluorescence stability compared to commercial dye, quinine. The effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are also compared, as shown in Fig. S4(d). The result reveals that both CDs@colistin-3 and CDs have same effect on the variation of the temperature.

The attachment of colistin was confirmed by 1HNMR analysis of CDs@colistin-3, CDs, and colistin, as shown in Fig. S1(a–c). The 1HNMR spectra of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3 shows the peak at the region 2.5–3.3ppm for the sp3 CH protons [24]. On the other hand, in the 1HNMR spectra of colistin, the peaks at 0.5–2.5ppm indicate the peaks for primary proton of colistin [49]. The similar peak at the region of 0.5–2.5ppmin 1HNMR spectra of CDs@colistin-3 demonstrated the attachment of the colistin to the surface of the CDs. Successively, ESI Mass spectra of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3, CDs, and colistin were carried out to further confirm the attachment of colistin to the surface of the CDs. The common peaks both for CDs@colistin-3 at m/z 385 and CDs at m/z 578 demonstrate that colistin was successfully attached to the CDs surface, as shown in Fig. S7(a–c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential measurement of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Zeta potential of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are studied at different pH, as shown in Fig. S5. The result indicates that both CDs@colistin-3 and CDs have high negatively charged functional group at pH 9. The increment of the zeta potential with decreasing pH indicates that the negatively charged functional groups at the surface of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs becomes neutralized by protons [50]. Further the zeta potential of CDs@colistin-3 and CDs are obtained at −10.01mV and −9.85mV respectively at pH 7. The slightly increase in zeta potential of CDs@colistin-3 is due to the passivation of the colistin to the surface of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@colistin-3 as probes for Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To identify selective fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ metal ions by the CDs@colistin-3 fluorescence probe, the quenching performance was carried out in the presence of 1mM different metal ions. The F/F0 plot in presence of 1mM different metal ions is shown in Fig. 4 (a). Where F indicates the fluorescence intensity in presence of metal ions and F0 indicates the fluorescence intensity in absence of metal ions at 360nm excitation. This plot reveals that the ratio F/F0 of Fe3+ is small compared to other metal ions. The synthesised CDs@colistin-3 shows intense fluorescence at 365nm UV light, leading to decrement of fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+ metal ions. The result corresponds that Fe3+ ion effectively quenches the fluorescence intensity. The synthesised CDs@colistin-3 containing many hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups [51], already proved by FTIR spectrum of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3. The hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups on the surface of CDs@colistin-3 preferably forming chelate with Fe3+ metal ion [52,53]. The chelating and charge or electron transfer can quench the fluorescence emission at 360nm excitation. The continuous decrement of the fluorescence emission has shown in Fig. 4(b). A plot of (F0 −F)/F0 with a different concentration of Fe3+ ions (0–270μM) shows a good linear relationship up to 48μM but after 48μM deviation occurred [Fig. 4(c)]. The plot of F0/F against different concentration of Fe3+ ion shows a linear relationship in the range 0–48μM with R2 value 0.998 is inserted in Fig. 4(c). Fluorescence spectra of CDs@colistin-3 were recorded in presence of only Fe3+ ion and Fe3+ with mixture of cations, as shown in Fig. 4(d). The result reveals that the influence of coexisting metal ions was negligible. Successively, The LOD value for the detection of Fe3+ was calculated to be 56nM at neutral pH. The obtained LOD values are comparable with other reported papers, as shown in Table S1.

We have compared the labeling efficiency of the four types of colistin modified CDs with a series of different concentration of E. coli bacteria cell, as shown in Fig. 5 . The efficiency of CDs@colistin-3 was greater than other three colistin drug conjugated CDs. The greater labeling efficiency of CDs@colistin-3 compared to CDs@colistin-1, CDs@colistin-2, and CDs@colistin-4 might be attributed to its high content of colistin molecules on the surface of CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@colistin-3 with a series of different concentration of E. coli shows good linear relation (R2 value 0.998), as shown in Fig. 5(f). The fluorescence emission intensity of E. coli labelled with CDs@colistin-3 shows linear relationship with increasing concentration of E. coli bacteria cell with limit of detection (LOD, at S/N ratio of 3) approximately 460 cfu/mL. The fluorescence emission intensity of E. coli labelled with CDs@colistin-1, CDs@colistin-2 and CDs@colistin-4 also increased with increasing concentration of E. coli bacteria cell with limit of detection (LOD, at S/N ratio 3) approximately 1024cfu/mL, 578cfu/mL, and 1038cfu/mL respectively. The Fig. S6 represents the variation of LOD with amounts of colistin used in the synthesis of colistin conjugated CDs. The higher value of LOD for the detection of E. coli was obtained by labeling with CDs@colistin-3 compared to CDs@colistin-1, CDs@colistin-2, and CDs@colistin-4, indicates the high contain of the colistin on the surface of CDs@colistin-3. The result of LOD is compared with some other previously reported papers based on optical bacterial sensors (Table S2).

The colistin unit of the colistin modified CDs basically interact with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) molecules in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria36. To determine the specificity of CDs@colistin-3 for the detection of gram negative bacteria (E. coli), the similar experiment was executed with gram positive bacterial cell of S. aureus. The fluorescence intensity of E. coli (1×108 cfu/mL) and S. aureus (1×108 cfu/mL) was recorded after the treatment with 0.1mL of CDs@colistin-3 (1mg/mL), as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The fluorescence intensity of S. aureus bacterial cell was performed at 360nm excitation after treatment with CDs@colistin-3. Comparatively poor fluorescence intense peak demonstrated that the CDs@colistin-3 was selective only for E. coli. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of E. coli (1×108 cfu/mL) separately treated with CDs@colistin-3 and CDs was recorded at 360nm excitation as shown in Fig. 6(b). Negligibly small fluorescence intensity peak of E. coli bacterial cell treated with CDs indicates that CDs@colistin-3 only bind towards E. coli surface. The fluorescence images of the E. coli and S. aureus treated with CDs@colistin-3 are shown in Fig. 7 . The fluorescence images of the E. coli and S. aureus show that E. coli exhibits bright image in the both filter [excitation wave length 450–480nm, 510–550nm] in compared to S. aureus.

The selective detection of Gram-positive bacteria in the real sample (human urine, apple juice, and tap water) was conducted with a series of different concentrated E. coli. The fluorescence intensity of the E. coli obtained from three different real samples was recorded at 360nm excitation after treatment with CDs@colistin-3. The fluorescence intensities of E. coli labelled with CDs@colistin-3 from the three different real samples were increased with increasing concentration of E. coli bacterial shell, as shown in Fig. S3. The detection limits for E. coli obtained from human urine, apple juice, and tap water (LOD; at S/N ratio of 3) are approximately 567cfu/mL, 640cfu/mL, 598 cfu/mL respectively. The results demonstrated that our colistin modified CDs can be effectively used for selective detection of E. coli in real sample.

In summary, we have successfully synthesised highly fluorescence colistin antibiotic drug conjugated nitrogen doped CDs by simple and single step pyrolysis method for selective labeling of gram negative bacteria. The synthesised colistin modified CDs can also be successfully used for monitoring Fe3+ ion. The synthesised CDs@colistin-3 exhibited good photo stability as well as monodispersity. The CDs@colistin-3 with high fluorescence (quantum yield 7.56%) has detected E. coli with effective LOD 460cfu/mL. Our synthesised CDs@colistin-3 can also be used to detect E. coli in real samples (human urine, apple juice, and tap water) with detection limit 567cfu/mL, 640 cfu/mL, 598 cfu/mL respectively. Moreover, our fluorescence sensor can also detect Fe3+ with linear range 0–48μM and LOD value 56nM. Due to good cell permeability and low cytotoxicity of the synthesised CDs@colistin-3, it can be successfully applied for labeling and imaging of E. coli bacteria cell.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank Prof. Somenath Roy, Department of Human Physiology with Community Health, Vidyasagar University for fluorescence imaging. This work is financially supported by DST, Government of India (SB/FT/CS-068/2013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Soumen Chandra received his B. Sc. (Chemistry) from The University of Burdwan, Indiain 2010, and M. Sc. (Chemistry) from Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, India in 2012. He is currently working as a senior research fellow at Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, India under the supervision of Dr. Sumanta Kumar Sahu. His research interests include the development of smart carbon dots and its applications in bioimaging and sensing/biosensing.

Triveni Kumar Mahto, received his Ph.D in catalysis from Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad in 2017. He completed M. Phil. in Applied Chemistry in 2009 from Indian School of Mines Dhanbad, India. Before M. Phil, he completed his M. Sc. and B. Ed. from Vinoba Bhave University, Hazaribag (India). Currently he is a lecturer in the Engineering Chemistry Department at Birsa Institute of Technology Sindri, Dhanbad (India). His research interests are focused on energy and environment: design and development of nanomaterials working against environmental pollution, biofuel generation, development of new strategies for alternating energy etc.

Angshuman Ray Chowdhuri received his B. Sc. (Chemistry) from The University of Burdwan, India in 2011, and M. Sc. (Chemistry) from Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, India in 2013. He is currently working as a senior research fellow at Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, India under the supervision of Dr. Sumanta Kumar Sahu. His research interests include the development functionalized nanomaterials and nanoscale metal organic frameworks for anticancer drug delivery, sensing/biosensing, and enzyme immobilization.

Balaram Das received his B. Sc. Degree in Physiology from Vidyasagar University, India in 2008, and M. Sc. Degree in Human Physiology from Vidyasagar University, India in 2010. He is currently working as a senior research scholar at Department of Human Physiology with Community Health, Vidyasagar University, India under the supervision of Prof. Somenath Roy. His research interests include the interface of chemistry and biology using nanotechnology as a tool to fight fatal disease like cancer and microbiological infections, synthesis of different metal nanoparticles and applications in anticancer and antimicrobial drug delivery.

Dr. Sumanta K. Sahu received his Ph.D in nano-medicine from Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur in 2011. He is currently the head of the research group ‘Functional Nanomaterials for biomedical applications’ at the Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad since 2012. He has co-authored over 40 scientific research papers. His research interests include nanomaterials, functional porous materials, and their applications in drug delivery, catalysis, sensing, and separation
